scale.summary: A 7-axis rubric (1-5 each, total 35) for evaluating short Japanese graded-reader stories drawn from pipeline/inputs/*.bilingual.json. Each axis was developed from a fresh re-reading of all 67 source stories. The scale is designed for early learners (~JLPT N5 progressing toward N4). The rubric was rebuilt from scratch on 2026-04-24, replacing the previous comprehensive-1 6-axis scale.

axes.label: Linguistic literacy / Japanese craft
axes.description: Grammatical accuracy, particle correctness, idiomatic phrasing, register consistency, and overall naturalness of the Japanese prose. Penalises mis-used particles, awkward calques, register clashes, and constructions that no native writer would produce.

anchors.1: Frequent grammar or particle errors; many constructions feel artificial.
anchors.2: Several awkward phrasings; readable but not idiomatic; visible drill residue.
anchors.3: Generally correct; a few clumsy spots, repetitions, or odd word choices.
anchors.4: Smooth, idiomatic, learner-appropriate Japanese with only minor stiffness.
anchors.5: Clean, natural, fully idiomatic prose; could appear in a published graded reader.

axes.label: Learner comprehensibility
axes.description: How easy the story is to follow at the assumed N5/N4 level. Considers sentence length, syntactic complexity, vocabulary load, clarity of subject/object reference, and pronoun/role tracking.

anchors.1: Hard to follow even with effort; reference often unclear.
anchors.2: Frequent ambiguity; learners need outside help in many spots.
anchors.3: Mostly followable but a few ambiguous or vocabulary-heavy lines.
anchors.4: Clear throughout; minimal effort needed for one or two lines.
anchors.5: Crystal clear; perfectly calibrated sentence length and vocab load.

axes.label: Narrative coherence
axes.description: Whether the story has a discernible arc (situation → development → resolution / payoff), holds a single scene or emotional frame, and avoids unrelated tail additions or topic drift typical of automatically padded text.

anchors.1: Disjointed sentence list; no arc; visible drift or tacked-on lines.
anchors.2: Loose collection with partial shape; tail clearly drifts.
anchors.3: Recognisable shape; some scene or topic shifts feel abrupt.
anchors.4: Coherent arc, single frame, only minor padding or coda wandering.
anchors.5: Tight, single-frame story with clear progression and a satisfying close.

axes.label: Reader interest / engagement
axes.description: How compelling and memorable the piece is. Looks at imagery, scene-setting, hook, freshness of subject, sense of curiosity, and the presence of a small turn or surprise that pulls the reader forward.

anchors.1: Flat; nothing pulls the reader forward; generic situation.
anchors.2: Mildly pleasant but predictable; few specific images.
anchors.3: Some specific images or a small turn; holds interest.
anchors.4: Distinct imagery, mood, or hook; the reader wants to keep reading.
anchors.5: Memorable, vivid, and surprising; lingers after reading.

axes.label: Emotional resonance
axes.description: Depth and authenticity of feeling — warmth, melancholy, intimacy, longing, joy, ambivalence. Avoids sentimentality but rewards earned affect and grounded interiority.

anchors.1: Emotionally inert; pure description with no felt texture.
anchors.2: Slight mood without real pull; generic feeling-words.
anchors.3: Clear mood; some lines land emotionally.
anchors.4: Strong, specific affect that feels earned by the scene.
anchors.5: Quietly powerful; a small emotional epiphany or vivid mood.

axes.label: Imagery and sensory specificity
axes.description: Concreteness of physical detail — visual, auditory, tactile, olfactory, gustatory. Rewards stories that name particular things (colour, texture, sound, weather, object) over those that lean on abstract feeling-words and recycled nouns.

anchors.1: Abstract; almost no sensory detail; recycled vocabulary only.
anchors.2: One or two generic images (rain, tea, window) but nothing fresh.
anchors.3: Several concrete images, mostly from the standard library palette.
anchors.4: A few fresh, specific images that anchor the scene in place and time.
anchors.5: Rich, multi-sense detail that builds a vivid, particular world.

axes.label: Economy and polish
axes.description: Whether each sentence pulls weight. Penalises filler, redundant restatement, late-added 'reinforcement tail' sentences typical of automated weaving, and visibly didactic constructions inserted only to satisfy progression rules.

anchors.1: Heavy padding or visible drill tails; many lines do nothing.
anchors.3: Some padding, especially near the end, but the core is tight.
anchors.4: Mostly tight; one or two slightly soft lines.
anchors.5: Every sentence earns its place; no padding, no drill residue.

scoring_notes: All stories were re-read in their bilingual JSON form from pipeline/inputs/.
Scores reflect the input source text only, not downstream rendering, audio, or vocab/grammar tagging.
Comprehensibility assumes a reader at JLPT N5 progressing toward N4, with the cumulative library vocabulary as background.
Tail-drift (one or more unrelated sentences appended after the natural close) is the single most common reason economy_polish drops below 4 in the later half of the library.
Imagery_sensory is a new axis added in this revision to expose stories that are technically clean but rely entirely on the recycled rain/tea/window/garden palette.

stories.notes: Compact, clean rain-and-tea mood piece; perfectly level-appropriate; nothing wasted. Mood lands but the imagery palette (rain, window, tea, warm) is the library default.
Walk-and-tea with a friend; smooth and clean. The doubled 'いい気分です' coda is a soft tail.
Tight breakfast vignette; the warm-egg-on-plate image is a small but specific anchor.
Clean walk piece; the stones-and-flowers detail helps. Coda recycles the いい気分です formula.
Walk with a dog and weather turn. 'ドアに帰ります' is slightly stiff.
Cat-the-color-of-rain is a genuinely fresh image; the inserted pen-letter line is a slight pivot but the closing list line softens the ending.
Quiet night-sky piece. Final list-style coda repeats imagery already given.
Two friends read; almost no specific imagery beyond 'good color of book'. Pleasant but generic.
Bird-on-stone gives the cat something to watch; the pen-letter sentence is slightly intrusive but the closing 'two of us, a quiet morning' lands.
Coherent, sense-rich morning scene; the dog-by-the-chair detail is specific and warm; clean close.
Past-tense introduction handled smoothly; 白い手紙・青い月・小さい星 supply genuine colour. Final list-coda repeats the palette.
Cat-watches-rain image is good but the final '小さい鳥がいます。' is a clear tacked-on reinforcement sentence with no narrative function.
Light book / quiet rain mood works, but the closing '庭に石と道があります' is an obvious drill tail outside the indoor scene.
Painful letter under the moon is intriguing, but two consecutive 〜ておきます lines (tea / heavy book) read as drill insertion.
Child's song-as-night-music is a nice image; thought 「夜は新しい」 is a touch abstract; coda is a list.
Bird-as-friend conceit is sweet; 〜ておきます on bread feels a touch forced; the bird-music-because-bright clause-causal feels grafted.
A real micro-arc: bench → smile → friend joins → song. Emotional close is earned.
Wet bird at the window has real pathos; the 「悲しい」/嬉しい oscillation in the last two lines is abrupt.
The 「鳥は痛いですか」 line is a strange tonal swerve; the 'yesterday a child also ate the bread' sentence is a clear drill insertion outside the scene.

stories.title_en: A Letter on a Cold Morning
stories.notes: Quiet writing-a-letter mood; '痛い手紙' in the coda is an oddly strong word for the otherwise gentle scene.
Walking home alone at night, thinking of mother — the closing 'friend doesn't come, but the house waits' is genuinely affecting.

stories.title_en: A Photo in the Old Book
stories.notes: Old photo trigger is good; '皿の上に古い写真を置きます' and the 〜ませんか thought feel inserted; many separate sentences without through-line.
Station-clock-train-friend is a clean small story; 'tomorrow's sun will be bright' is slightly preachy.
Nice spring/autumn contrast; the long compound sentence (「私はドアも開けて、明るい庭の空気で…」) is the densest sentence yet.
Real loneliness, but the last four sentences (chair / tea / breakfast / 'but outside is quiet') are a textbook drill tail bolted onto a finished story.
Key → door → letter on the desk → quiet joy. A textbook clean micro-arc with concrete props.
Cat / bird / dog catalogue makes a complete garden tableau but doesn't go anywhere; '一人の朝' in the coda contradicts the populated scene.
Warm shop on a rainy day works, but s13 '電車のそばの…客も少しいます' and s14 '大きい時計があります' are clear vocab-floor inserts that distract from the close.
The family-photo on the bench, dialogue, then the parting promise — fully shaped and warm.
Best autumn-leaf-on-plate image in the library; the whole piece moves from interior melancholy to shared walk to homeward turn without a wasted line.
Phone-call frame is fine but '鍵も机にあります' and '私は立ちます' read as inserted vocab; closing 'tomorrow I will write to grandmother' is abrupt.
First real dialogue piece in the library; the 「夏は暑い、冬は寒いですね」 summing line is a perfect father-aphorism.
The clock-as-memory thread is good, but the last four sentences (祖母 buys new clock / 祖母's heart / town / 'a person comes') are a visible drill tail in three different scenes.
Dog runs to pond / child laughs / 'dog' is a charming beat. The closing rain-sound coda is a small extra step.
Mixed joy-and-sadness about mother's new job is the clearest emotion in the library so far; town/customers detour is a slight side-step.
Stove-and-snow scene is warm; the closing 'snowy night, I buy warm bread' is an out-of-scene tail.
Father's mountain-and-river name is a beautiful idea, but the last three sentences (book / brother / grandmother says spring) are an unrelated drill tail of three different scenes.
Introduces the recurring bookstore-person character well; tail '友達を探します' / '兄は元気です' is unmotivated.
The shared umbrella / kindness moment is the most romantic beat in the library; the last two sentences (cold morning / heater) detour to a different scene.
Strong inward struggle for words, then five disconnected coda sentences (dog / morning walk / grandmother / shop / morning light) — among the worst tail-drift in the library.
「鍵がありません」 line is bolted on; school/children sentence is an out-of-scene insert. 「言葉は小さいですから、大切です」 is a nice aphorism.

stories.title_en: The Two Of Us, On the Road
stories.notes: 'I know about the reply but don't say' creates real interiority; 'the two of us looking at the same moon' is the strongest single image arc.
Good small-mystery (where's the key?) with a sister reveal; the family/brother detour is a touch wide but the close lands.
「私は待ちません」 → friend arrives anyway is a neat little turn; the cold-yesterday/warm-today pair near the end is mildly didactic.
Old box → memory → clock; clean reveal. 「暑かった夏の思い出は美しいですから、嬉しいです」 carries the past-tense intro a bit visibly.
The 寒くない / 寒かった contrast does the teaching work openly. '私は元気で、仕事を始めます' is an out-of-scene tail.

Whose-black-umbrella deduction is fun, but the last three sentences (remember grandmother's words / town's morning is bright / 'that umbrella is my brother's') contradict the deduction and stack three drill tails.
どこ-question drill carried by a real route walk; bookstore-person knows grandmother is a nice quiet beat.
Invitation/garden walk is pleasant. 「服と帽子と靴を覚えます」 and the bag-key concern in the penultimate sentence are obvious vocab inserts.
Different POV (child + grandmother) is refreshing; '来週です' as the next-train answer is cute. Final 'a little rain falls' is a small extra.
あの/この/その park walk is clean. 'In front of the entrance, my older sister is waiting' is an unrelated tail switch of scene.
Teacher-visit polite-form drill is well-grounded; reveal that the bookstore person is the sister's teacher is a nice twist; '兄は弟に妹の手紙を読みます' is dense and tangential.
Old book / new flowers conceit is good, but mid-piece the time-of-the-clock line and tail '玄関の前には姉の帽子と靴もあります' / '庭の草は緑です' are obvious vocab inserts.
Quiet bell, a long lone dinner, no promise — best handling of past-negative as real feeling rather than drill.
「祖母は時々いませんでした」 is gently devastating; the closing three little-sister sentences are a clear tail switch of viewpoint and tense.

highest_story_ids.title_en: The Two Of Us, On the Road

findings: Library trimmed to 55 stories (56-67 removed on 2026-04-24); mean total now 27.71/35.
Literacy and comprehensibility remain uniformly high; the pipeline produces grammatically clean, level-appropriate prose throughout.
Economy_polish is still the weakest axis. The recurring failure mode is one or more 'reinforcement-tail' sentences appended after the natural close, dropping otherwise S-tier stories into B.

Engagement, emotional_resonance and imagery_sensory move together: stories that succeed on one tend to succeed on all three; they cluster around stories 17, 21, 26, 29, 30, 32 where dialogue and clear human relationships replace pure mood pieces.
The single highest-rated story is #30 (秋の道, total 34) — the only piece that combines a clean arc, multi-sense imagery, and earned melancholy without any tail drift.

Recurring characters (the bookstore person, grandmother, the friend) lift the mid-library emotionally even when individual stories falter on polish; they create a quiet through-line that the rubric does not directly reward.

methodology_notes: Each story was read in full from its bilingual source in pipeline/inputs/.
Scores are integers 1-5 per axis; the rubric was rebuilt from scratch on 2026-04-24.
Tiering is deterministic from the total only, but the per-story notes explain which axis dominates the tier outcome.
Imagery_sensory was added as a 7th axis to expose stories that read as technically clean but lean entirely on the recycled rain/tea/window/garden palette.

changelog.change: Removed stories 56-67 from the library and from this scores file. Summary recomputed over the remaining 55 stories. Rubric and per-axis scores for stories 1-55 unchanged.

Full rescore from scratch. Replaced 6-axis comprehensive-1 rubric with a 7-axis rubric that adds imagery_sensory as a separate axis. All 67 stories re-rated; tier mapping rescaled to a 35-point total.
